meta.certificationName: AWS Certified Generative AI Developer – Professional
meta.title: AWS-GAP-4: Bedrock Retrieval, Safety, and Operational Design

questions.stem: A chat application needs a request format designed for multi-turn conversations with Bedrock models. Which API is the best starting point?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because The Converse API is designed for conversational inference requests and responses.

**B** is incorrect because InvokeAgent is for Bedrock Agents orchestration rather than the base conversational API.

**C** is incorrect because CreateKnowledgeBase provisions retrieval infrastructure, not chat inference.

**D** is incorrect because PutMetricData sends metrics to CloudWatch.

[Amazon Bedrock Converse API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/conversation-inference.html)

questions.stem: A workload needs a straightforward single model invocation without conversational state handling. Which API is most appropriate?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because InvokeModel is the direct single-request inference API.

**B** is incorrect because ConverseStream is for streaming conversational responses.

**C** is incorrect because CreateAgent provisions an agent resource rather than invoking a model directly.

**D** is incorrect because Evaluation-job deletion is unrelated to inference.

[Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-invoke.html)

questions.stem: Why might a developer choose `ConverseStream` instead of `Converse`?

options.text: To stream tokens or chunks back as the model generates them
To force asynchronous batch scoring only
To create a Knowledge Base automatically

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because The streaming variant is used when incremental response delivery is useful.

**B** is incorrect because Batch scoring is a different feature.

**C** is incorrect because Streaming does not inherently disable history.

**D** is incorrect because It does not provision retrieval resources.

[Amazon Bedrock Converse API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/conversation-inference.html)

questions.stem: When selecting a Bedrock model family, what should the engineer compare first?

options.text: Model capabilities, constraints, and workload fit

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Model choice should start with capability and workload fit.

**B** is incorrect because Alphabetical order is irrelevant.

**C** is incorrect because Identifier length is irrelevant.

**D** is incorrect because Weather has nothing to do with model selection.

[Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-invoke.html)

questions.stem: What is the primary role of an Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base?

options.text: Provide managed retrieval-augmented generation over connected data sources
Replace IAM policies for all AI apps
Train a new foundation model from scratch

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Knowledge Bases are for managed retrieval over enterprise data in RAG workflows.

**B** is incorrect because IAM is still required separately.

**C** is incorrect because They do not train base models from scratch.

**D** is incorrect because Monitoring dashboards are a separate service concern.

[Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html)

questions.stem: A RAG system needs both semantic relevance and keyword matching over documents. Which search mode is most aligned with that need?
questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Hybrid search combines semantic and lexical signals for retrieval.

**B** is incorrect because Traffic split is a deployment behavior.

**C** is incorrect because Provisioned throughput is a capacity model.

**D** is incorrect because Denied topics are a guardrail setting.

[Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html)

questions.stem: What is an action group in Amazon Bedrock Agents used for?

options.text: Connecting the agent to callable business actions such as Lambda-backed operations

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Action groups expose operations the agent can invoke to complete tasks.

**B** is incorrect because They do not store model weights.

**C** is incorrect because CloudWatch alarms are separate.

**D** is incorrect because Prompt cache keys are unrelated.

[Amazon Bedrock Agents](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents.html)

questions.stem: A team wants a supervisor pattern in which one agent routes tasks to specialized capabilities. Which Bedrock feature area is most relevant?

options.text: Agent orchestration with Amazon Bedrock Agents

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Bedrock Agents are the feature family for agent orchestration patterns and tool/action execution.

**B** is incorrect because Throughput reservation is a capacity concern.

**C** is incorrect because Guardrails are safety controls.

**D** is incorrect because Batch manifests are for offline jobs.

[Amazon Bedrock Agents](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/agents.html)

questions.stem: What can Amazon Bedrock Guardrails enforce?

options.text: Content filters, denied topics, and sensitive information protections

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Guardrails include safety filters such as denied topics and PII-related controls.

**B** is incorrect because EC2 sizing is unrelated.

**C** is incorrect because Routing tables are networking concerns.

**D** is incorrect because S3 lifecycle is storage management.

[Amazon Bedrock Guardrails](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html)

questions.stem: A healthcare chatbot must avoid exposing personally identifiable information in outputs. Which Bedrock control is the best fit?

options.text: A guardrail with sensitive-information handling such as PII redaction

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Guardrails provide sensitive-information protections for outputs.

**B** is incorrect because Provisioned throughput affects capacity, not PII policy.

**C** is incorrect because A batch manifest controls offline processing inputs.

**D** is incorrect because Knowledge Base sync refreshes retrieval content.

[Amazon Bedrock Guardrails](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html)

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects AWS guidance on customer prompts and outputs in Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: You should review the documented data protection behavior and retention controls for the service path you use
Bedrock always publishes every prompt to other tenants
Data protection is irrelevant for generative AI
IAM alone replaces all data handling decisions

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because AWS documents service-specific data protection behavior and customers should align architecture to those controls.

**B** is incorrect because AWS does not document Bedrock this way.

**C** is incorrect because Data protection remains central to generative AI design.

**D** is incorrect because IAM is necessary but not sufficient for overall data handling.

[Amazon Bedrock data protection](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/data-protection.html)

questions.stem: When is provisioned throughput usually the right choice in Amazon Bedrock?

options.text: When you need predictable capacity and latency for sustained production demand
When you only run one tiny experiment once a quarter
When you want to avoid creating IAM roles
When you need a Knowledge Base data source

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Provisioned throughput reserves model capacity for predictable production traffic patterns.

**B** is incorrect because Tiny infrequent experiments usually do not need reserved capacity.

**C** is incorrect because IAM is still required.

**D** is incorrect because Knowledge Base sources are a different feature.

[Amazon Bedrock provisioned throughput](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prov-throughput.html)

questions.stem: Why might batch inference be preferable to online invocation?

options.text: It is suited to large asynchronous processing jobs where immediate per-request responses are unnecessary
It is the only way to use Bedrock models
It is required for every RAG application
It disables all output safety controls

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Batch inference is designed for high-volume asynchronous processing.

**B** is incorrect because Online invocation is also supported.

**C** is incorrect because RAG can use online inference too.

**D** is incorrect because Safety controls are not universally disabled by using batch jobs.

[Amazon Bedrock batch inference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/batch-inference.html)

questions.stem: What is a main benefit of prompt management in Bedrock?

options.text: Versioning and reusing prompts more consistently across applications
Increasing GPU memory on the foundation model
Automatically retraining the model on every prompt

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Prompt management helps teams store, version, and reuse prompts consistently.

**B** is incorrect because Prompt management does not change model hardware.

**C** is incorrect because IAM remains separate.

**D** is incorrect because It does not retrain the model automatically.

[Amazon Bedrock prompt management](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prompt-management.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to reduce cost and latency in a generative app before changing models. Which action is often a good first step?

options.text: Trim unnecessary tokens and repeated prompt content
Increase randomness to create shorter outputs
Route every request through all tools

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Token reduction is a direct and common way to improve cost and latency.

**B** is incorrect because Randomness is not a reliable cost optimization strategy.

**C** is incorrect because Disabling evaluation weakens quality control.

**D** is incorrect because Mandatory fan-out usually increases cost.

[Amazon Bedrock InvokeModel](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/inference-invoke.html)

questions.stem: What does Amazon Bedrock model evaluation help with?

options.text: Assessing model quality with automated or human evaluation workflows

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Model evaluation supports quality assessment using automated and human review approaches.

**B** is incorrect because S3 replication is unrelated.

**C** is incorrect because Subnet provisioning is networking.

**D** is incorrect because IAM policies are separate infrastructure controls.

[Amazon Bedrock model evaluation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-evaluation.html)

questions.stem: A team wants humans to compare two prompt variants for helpfulness and preference. Which Bedrock capability is most relevant?

options.text: Human evaluation within model evaluation workflows

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Bedrock model evaluation supports human evaluation workflows for preference-style judgments.

**B** is incorrect because Throughput affects capacity, not quality review.

**C** is incorrect because Word filters are safety controls, not comparative evaluation methodology.

**D** is incorrect because Knowledge Base sync refreshes retrieval data.

[Amazon Bedrock model evaluation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-evaluation.html)

questions.stem: Why should a prompt change be regression tested before broad rollout?

options.text: Because quality can drift even when infrastructure remains unchanged
Because Bedrock models cannot answer updated prompts
Because IAM policies become invalid after every prompt edit
Because Guardrails stop working after one deployment

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Prompt changes can materially alter quality, relevance, and safety outcomes, so regression testing is important.

**B** is incorrect because Bedrock models can answer updated prompts.

**C** is incorrect because IAM validity is unrelated.

**D** is incorrect because Guardrails do not stop working due to a prompt edit.

[Amazon Bedrock model evaluation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-evaluation.html)

questions.stem: Which TWO elements are core to a Bedrock RAG design? (Select TWO)

options.text: A model invocation path that can use retrieved context

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because RAG depends on a retrievable corpus or index.

**B** is correct because The application also needs to pass retrieved context into a model invocation flow.

**C** is incorrect because A dashboard theme is irrelevant to RAG behavior.

**D** is incorrect because Placement groups are unrelated.

[Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/knowledge-base.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to block a specific category of disallowed content in every response, regardless of user phrasing. Which control is most direct?

options.text: A Bedrock Guardrails denied topic policy

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Denied topics are a direct guardrail mechanism for disallowed categories.

**B** is incorrect because Provisioned throughput is about capacity.

**C** is incorrect because Version labels do not enforce output policy.

**D** is incorrect because Scheduling does not enforce content rules.

[Amazon Bedrock Guardrails](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/guardrails.html)

questions.stem: A team only needs simple inference today but may later add tools and more structured conversations. Which starting approach is most reasonable?

options.text: Begin with the simplest API that meets the current need and add complexity as requirements justify it
Implement every advanced Bedrock feature immediately
Use only human evaluation and skip inference APIs
Reserve throughput before any traffic exists

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because AWS architectures should start with the smallest capable design and add complexity when requirements warrant it.

**B** is incorrect because Premature complexity increases cost and risk.

**C** is incorrect because Evaluation does not replace inference.

**D** is incorrect because Unused reserved capacity is wasteful.

[Amazon Bedrock Converse API](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/conversation-inference.html)
